title: Lecture Template
text:
Introduction
These slides are created using revealjs format in Quarto.

We’ve highlighted many of the useful and cool features, but you can explore even more on the Quarto website:

revealjs (Presentations in Quarto)
Authoring in Quarto (markdown basics & content formatting)
Quarto: The Definitive Guide (a book about Quarto created using Quarto

title: Lecture Template
text:
Font Styles
italics, bold, bold italics, strikethrough, monospaced

Bigger font


Smaller font


This text will appear red.

This is line green text.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Math
You can write mathematical equations using LaTex

e.g. $f(y) = x^2$ renders to \(f(y) = x^2\)

You can center math on its own line using double dollar signs, e.g. $$f(y) = x^2$$ renders to:
\[f(y) = x^2\]

title: Lecture Template
text:
Tables

Table caption: Math syntax summary


Syntax
Appearance
Usage




$...$
Inline
For short expressions in text


$$...$$
Display
For emphasizing key equations

title: Lecture Template
text:
Animated Bullets
You can create incremental lists using the incremental div.


Source
::: incremental
-   la
-   la
-   land
:::

Rendered

la
la
land



Note: You can also enable this for all lists by default:
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: true

title: Lecture Template
text:
Writing Code
To include runnable code in Quarto, use fenced code blocks with the appropriate language engine:

```{r}
# Load data
data(mtcars)

# Fit linear model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
```


Use three backticks followed by {r} to start an R code block.
You can add Chunk Options after #|, one per line.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Chunk Options
These control how code blocks behave:

echo: true/false – Show or hide the source code.
eval: true/false – Evaluate the code or not.
code-fold: true/false – Allow the code block to be collapsible.
code-summary: "text" – Customize label for folded code.



These are just a few popular examples—Quarto supports more chunk options.
➡️ Full reference: https://quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options.html

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Folding
See the next slide for the rendered output …
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-summary: "Click me to show the code!"
#| code-fold: true

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Folding


Click me to show the code!
library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Hiding
Setting echo: false hides the code and shows only the output.
```{r}
#| echo: false

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```
See the next slide for the rendered output …

title: Lecture Template
text: Example: Code Hiding

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code Copying
All code blocks include a clipboard icon by default. Hover and click to copy the code.

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Output Location
By default, the output will follow immediately after the code. Use output-location to control where the output appears:







output-location
Description




fragment
Output appears as a fragment—revealed when you advance the slide


slide
Output appears on the next slide, separate from the code


column
Code and output shown side‑by‑side in two equal-width columns on one slide


column-fragment
Same as column, but output is delayed as a fragment

title: Lecture Template
text:
Adjusting Slide Size
The table didn’t quite fit on the previous slide, so we use the .smaller CSS class to reduce the font size for the entire slide. Here’s what’s happening under the hood:
## Normal Slide

This is normal size text.

## Smaller Slide {.smaller}

This text will be smaller so more fits on the slide.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Default (output after code)

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Output on next slide

```{r}
#| output-location: slide

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text: Example: Output on next slide

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Column


```{r}
#| output-location: column

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Resize figure


```{r}
#| output-location: column
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 3
#| out-width: "100%"

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Scrollable Slides
The .scrollable class makes overflow content scrollable vertically rather than being cut off.
You can acheive it using:
## Slide Title {.scrollable}
This is especially useful when you have:

Long code blocks
Large tables
Verbose output
Lists or explanations that extend beyond the slide height

title: Lecture Template
text:
Smaller Slides
The .smaller class—used in the same way as .scrollable— reduces the base font size for the entire slide, excluding the slide title.
## Normal Slide

This is normal size text.

## Smaller Slide {.smaller}

This text will be smaller so more fits on the slide.
An example of this can be seen on this slide.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Cross-referencing slides

Given the following slide title, Quarto will automatically generate the ID: of my-amazing-slide.1
## My Amazing Slide! 
To reference that slide we can use

[this slide](#my-amazing-slide), e.g. this slide
[My Amazing Slide!], e.g. Output Location


Special characters are removed, spaces become dashes, and uppercase letters are converted to lowercase.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Custom Titles
If you want to make a custom-id, use:
## This slide title is too long {#short-id}
and reference using:
[link](#short-id) 

Quarto auto-generates heading IDs (like Pandoc) by: converting to lower case, removing punctation and replacing spaces with hyphens
How to manually assign IDs using {#my-id}
How these IDs can be used as anchors
This slide is here to show how to make custom slide ids

title: Lecture Template
text:
Whats Ahead
The above should be more than you need to get started creating presentations in your course. If you’re building more customized or interactive slides, we cover:

Fragments & animations
Slide state events
Custom keyboard bindings
Code execution and plugins
Embedding other HTML/JS content

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code highlighting
In addition to the HTML Code Block options, revealjs supports the ability to do line highlighting. This is accomplished using:
#| code-line-numbers: "1-2|4-5|7-8"

# Load data
data(mtcars)

# Fit linear model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

# Summary output
summary(model)  



See Quarto: Line Highlighting

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code Annotation
Much like footnotes, we could use Code Annotations to attach explanation to lines of code.

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
1penguins |>
2  mutate(
    bill_ratio = bill_depth_mm / bill_length_mm,
    bill_area  = bill_depth_mm * bill_length_mm
  )


1

Take penguins, and then,

2

add new columns for the bill ratio and bill area.






Source: https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/code-annotation.html

title: Lecture Template
Lecture Template
text:
Code Animations II
You can also animate between code blocks to show changes in code. For example:
# Define a server for the Shiny app
function(input, output) {
  
  # Fill in the spot we created for a plot
  output$phonePlot <- renderPlot({
    # Render a barplot
    barplot(WorldPhones[,input$region]*1000, 
            main=input$region,
            ylab="Number of Telephones",
            xlab="Year")
  })
}

Learn more: Code Animations

title: Lecture Template
text:
Visual Editor
The Visual Editor (VE) (as opposed to the Source Editor) in RStudio is a WYSIWYM editor interface.
It includes a toolbar with buttons and menu options for:

formatting text (bold, italics, headers, lists)
inserting tables, images
hyperlinks,
and more!

Bonus: It also provides previews of rendered math, plots, and supports the execution of code cells.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Figures/Images using VE
Quarto supports a number of ways to include figures and subfigures into your documents; see Quarto:Figures.

This is how you can included an image in yours slides using the Visual Editor in RStudio

title: Lecture Template
text:
Figure Options

The VE will open up a pop-up window where you can choose the alignment of your image (default, left, center, right), add captions, and include alternative text.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Right-aligned Image
For demonstration we make our image left-aligned.

In the Source Editor, the markdown syntax is:
![](img/logo.png){fig-align="right"}

title: Lecture Template
Lecture Template
text:
Resizing in Visual Editor

In VE you can resize an image by clicking and dragging its corner handles.
By default, the aspect ratio is locked. You can disable this and adjust dimensions independently.
See the next slide for a demonstration

title: Lecture Template
text:
Videos
You can embed videos in documents using {{< video >}} the shortcode; see Quarto: videos

For example, here we embed a YouTube video (see the rendered output on the following slide):
{{< video https://youtu.be/EbAAmrB0luA?si=f0TtOI9v_kotj8bk 
  width="800" 
  aspect-ratio="21x12" 
  >}}

title: Lecture Template
text:
iFrame
You can also embed videos/webpages using an iFrame








Note


Some websites block iframe, so previews may not appear in the rendered Quarto output for those sites.

title: Regression
text:
Introduction


In this lecture we will introduce Simple Linear Regression (SLR) to model the relationship between two numerical variables.
We will quantify the relationship between variables using popular metrics like the Correlation Coefficient and \(R^2\) value.
Statistical inference for this model will be discussed, and the critical distinction between inference for mean response and inference for the outcome will be clarified.
We will also introduce a basic understanding of the multiple regression model.

title: Regression
text:
Recall: Scatterplots

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Regression
text: Line of Best fit

title: Regression
text: Visualization of Residuals

title: Regression
text:
What is regression

Regression analysis is a tool to investigate how two or more variables are related.

“The general objective of a regression analysis is to determine the relationship between two (or more) variables so that we can gain information about one of them through knowing values of the other(s).”


Source: Ch 12 of Devore, Berk, and Carlton (2021)

title: Regression
text:
Variable Types
We want to see how a specific response variable, \(Y\) is affected by one or more explanatory variables \(X_1, \dots, X_p\)

\(Y\) is also known as the dependent variable or output variable. This variable is continuous (i.e. numeric data type)
\(X_i\)’s are also known as independent/predictor variables or features. These may be continuous or categorical; however, we only consider continuous predictors in this course.

title: Regression
text:
Example



One may wish to use a person’s height, gender and race to predict a person’s weight. What is/are the response variable(s)?





Height
Gender
Race
Weight

title: Regression
text:
Example



One may wish to use a person’s height, gender and race to predict a person’s weight. What is/are the dependent variable(s)?





Height
Gender
Race
Weight

title: Regression
Regression
text:
Model
\[\begin{align}
Y &= \underbrace{f(X_1, \dots, X_p)}_{\text{deterministic}} + \underbrace{\epsilon}_{\text{random}}\\
&= \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots \beta_p X_p + \epsilon
\end{align}\]

In a linear regression model, the function \(f(X_1, \dots, X_p)\) is a linear combination of the explanatory variables.

title: Regression
text:
Model
\[\begin{align}
\overset{\overset{\text{response}}{\downarrow}}{Y} &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_1} + \beta_2 \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_2} + \dots \beta_p \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_p} + \epsilon
\end{align}\]


\(\beta_0\) is the \(y\)-intercept
\(\beta_i\) are the regression coefficients, and
\(\epsilon\) are the errors that are assumed \(N(0, \sigma)\)



In statistics, we can describe how variables are related using a mathematical function.
The function along with other assumptions is called a model.
There are many models we can consider. In this class, we will focus on linear models, particularly, when there is only one predictor variable.
We refer to this model as the simple linear regression model. (Penn STat 500)


as well as modeling numerical response variables using a numerical or categorical explanatory variable.

title: Regression
text:
Simple Linear Regression

When \(p = 1\), namely, we are only considering one explanatory variable, our model is called “simple” linear regression (SLR).
When \(p>1\) we fall into multiple linear regression (MLR).

title: Regression
text:
SLR model
The SLR model reduces to an equation for of a line:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X\]
where:

\(\beta_0\) is the \(y\)-intercept, and
\(\beta_1\) is the slope

title: Regression
text:
Simulation: Part 1

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

This is only the deterministic portion

title: Regression
text:
Comment

We have only code the deterministic portion of this model
In other words, we are missing random component
Let’s add some random noise …

title: Regression
text:
Simulation: Part II






Warning


Change sd as needed




Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Regression
text:
\(X\)sParametersDeterministicNoise


Create n values for x, for example using:

x = runif(n, min = -2, max = 10) # or runif() / rnorm()












For \(\beta_0\) = beta0 = 3, \(\beta_1\) = beta1 = 4, we have
\[Y = 3 + 4 \cdot X\]

title: Regression
text:
References




Devore, J. L., K. N. Berk, and M. A. Carlton. 2021. Modern Mathematical Statistics with Applications. Springer Texts in Statistics. Springer International Publishing. https://books.google.ca/books?id=ghcsEAAAQBAJ.

title: Course Title
text:
Lecture Slide
The slide decks above were made in Quarto in revealjs (HTML). A lecture template with some helpful demonstrations can be here; see the rendered version here.






Note



Note that revealjs presentations can be presented as HTML slides or can be printed to PDF for easier distribution. 🎉



Print/Save to PDF:
Reveal presentations can be exported to PDF via a special print stylesheet.

Toggle into Print View using the E key (or using the Navigation Menu)
Open the in-browser[^2] print dialog (CTRL/CMD+P).
Change the Destination setting to Save as PDF.
Change the Layout to Landscape.
Change the Margins to None.
Enable the Background graphics option.
Click Save 🎉

Visit https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs/ for more details on create revealjs slides.

title: In-Class Activity: Summarizing Numerical Data
text: This script will provide a quick demonstration of summarizing data visually.

title: In-Class Activity: Summarizing Numerical Data
text:
Pixar Data

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The data we will be looking at contains information about Pixar films released prior to 2021, as provided by Wikipedia. The following variables were collected for each film:

film: The name of the film.
release_date: The date the film premiered.
run_time: The length of the film in minutes.
rotten_tomatoes: Score from the review-aggregation website Rotten Tomatoes; scored out of 100.
box_office_worldwide: Box office gross amount in U.S. dollars worldwide.
budget: Movie budget in U.S. dollars.

Descriptive Statistics
The following functions are useful for summarizing numerical data: mean, median, min, max, range, sd, var, fivenum
Try them here:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Basic Plots
There is a generic plot function in R which is useful for creating quick visualizations of a data set. If you pass one numeric variable into this function, a quick plot will be made by creating a point for each observation in the order they occur in the data frame (index). Passing two numeric variables into this function will create a scatterplot, with the first variable corresponding to the x-axis and the second corresponding to the y-axis.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There are some arguments we can add to the plot function to make our visualizations more informative:

main = “Title of the Graph”
xlab = “x-axis label”
ylab = “y-axis label”
xlim = c(0,100) (changing the values on the x-axis)
ylim = c(0,100) (changing the values on the y-axis)
col = “colour” (full list of colours can be found here)
pch = number (list of potential shapes for points can be found here)

We can even add text to our plots by incorporating the text function.


Barplots
To draw a basic bar graph, we can simply use the barplot function and specify which variable it is that we want to represent the height of the bars. For example, we can use the following code to quickly plot the Rotten Tomatoes score for each of the Pixar movies:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There are some arguments we can add to the barplot function to make our bar graph more informative.

horiz = T (draw the bars horizontally)
name = variable to label the bars
las = 2 (rotates the labels)
cex.names = 0.5 (change the labels font size)



Histograms
We can create histograms of the data using the hist function. For example, we can use the following code to quickly create a histogram of the Rotten Tomatoes scores for the Pixar movies:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Arguments we can add to the hist function to make our graph more informative.

breaks = number (number of bars displayed)
labels = T (add numbers to bars)



Boxplots
The boxplot function can be used to (unsurprisingly) create boxplots. Keeping with our Rotten Tomatoes examples:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Arguments we can add to the boxplot function to make our graph more informative.

horizontal = T (draw the boxplot horizontally)

title: Tutorial: Regression Analaysis with R
text:
This is a sample lecture on regression. For this example, we set packages: ["tibble", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "palmerpenguins", "mosaic].


This example is on the palmerpenguins data set.





Let’s examine the data set. For simplicity, we will use complete cases for this tutorial.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



We would like to perform a t-test on the body mass of male and female Adelie penguins. Let’s start with some initial data analysis.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
let’s examine side-by-side boxplots and histograms of the mass of male and female Adelie penguins. For simplicity, we’ll remove the data points where information is missing.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Let’s conduct the following test on whether the mean body mass of male Adelie penguins is equal to the mean body mass of female Adelie penguins, or whether there is a difference.
The null and alternative hypothesis are \[
  H_{0}: \mu_{\text{male}} = \mu_{\text{female}}
  \quad
  H_{a}: \mu_{\text{male}} \neq \mu_{\text{female}}
\]

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Recall the simple linear regression model is
\[
Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} X + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)
\] This is estimated using \[
\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_{0} + \hat{\beta}_{1}x
\]
For the Adelie penguins, let’s examine whether there is a linear relationship between the bill_length and body_mass_g of the Adelie penguins. We’ll start with a scatterplot of the data.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There appears to be a positive linear relationship between body mass and flipper length. Let’s get the least squares regression line:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The equation of the regression line is:
\[
  \hat{y}_{\text{flipper length}} = 165.2
    + 0.007 x_{\text{mass}}
\]
Let’s superimpose the least squares line on the plot:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Let’s see if there is a difference between the flipper length of male and female flipper lengths:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There appers to be a difference for male and female penguins. Let’s create a new model with sex as an indicator variable.
\[
  \hat{y}_{\text{flipper length}} = \hat{\beta}_0
    + \hat{\beta}_{\text{mass}} x_{\text{mass}}
    + \hat{\beta}_{\text{sex}} x_{\text{sex}}
\] where
\[
x_{\text{sex}} =
  \begin{cases}
    1, \text{ if sex is } \texttt{male}; \\
    0, \text{ if sex is } \texttt{female}
  \end{cases}
\]

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
From the output above, we see our model is
\[
  \hat{y}_{\text{flipper length}} = 165.2
    + 0.006 x_{\text{mass}}
    + 0.344 x_{\text{sex}}
\]
If we add both lines to the scatter plot we get the following.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
From both the plot and the R output, we observe the itnteraction effect is not significant.

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
text:
Q3 — Find the average
Compute the mean of the vector c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10).
Your answer should be stored in a variable called avg_val.

#| exercise: ex_mean
#| exercise.lines: 2
#| echo: false
avg_val <- NULL


Use the mean() function: mean(c(…))


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_mean
#| solution: true
avg_val <- mean(c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10))




#| exercise: ex_mean
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  if (!exists("avg_val", envir = .envir_result)) {
    fail("You need to define `avg_val`.")
  }
  v <- get("avg_val", envir = .envir_result)
  if (!is.numeric(v) || length(v) != 1) {
    fail("`avg_val` should be a single numeric value.")
  }
  if (!isTRUE(all.equal(v, 6, tol = 1e-8))) {
    fail("Not quite — the mean of c(2,4,6,8,10) is 6.")
  }
  pass("✅ Correct! You computed the mean successfully.")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q4 — Draw a histogram from ToothGrowth (type the full call)
text:
Q4 — Draw a histogram from ToothGrowth (type the full call)
Use exactly one of these numeric columns and nothing else: len or dose



Photo by The Humble Co. on Unsplash








Preview



Find a preview of the ToothGrowth dataset here:

#| echo: true
head(ToothGrowth, 8)





#| exercise: ex_hist_exact
# Type your answer on the next line (one command only):


Use the hist command.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_hist_exact
#| solution: true
hist(ToothGrowth$len)



#| exercise: ex_hist_exact
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
code <- .user_code
lines <- strsplit(paste(code, collapse = "\n"), "\n", fixed = TRUE)[[1]]
lines <- sub("#.*$", "", lines)
trim <- function(s) sub("^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$", "", s)
lines <- trim(lines); lines <- lines[nzchar(lines)]

if (length(lines) == 0) fail("Type your answer; it can't be empty.")
if (length(lines) > 1)  fail("Enter exactly one command.")

one <- lines[1]
if (!inherits(.result, "histogram")) fail("Your code should draw a histogram.")

expr <- try(parse(text = one)[[1]], silent = TRUE)
if (inherits(expr, "try-error")) fail("Your code must be a single valid R expression.")
if (!(is.call(expr) && identical(as.character(expr[[1]]), "hist"))) fail("Use hist(...).")

args <- as.list(expr)[-1]
if (length(args) != 1L) fail("Pass exactly one argument to hist().")

arg_txt <- paste0(deparse(args[[1]]), collapse = "")
allowed <- c("ToothGrowth$len","ToothGrowth$dose")

if (arg_txt %in% allowed) pass("OK")
else if (arg_txt == "ToothGrowth") fail("hist(ToothGrowth) won’t work—choose len or dose.")
else fail("Type exactly hist(ToothGrowth$len) or hist(ToothGrowth$dose).")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q5 — Draw a boxplot from ToothGrowth (type the full call)

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q6 — Add a color to the histogram
text:
Q6 — Add a color to the histogram
Re-draw a histogram of ToothGrowth$len and set any bar color using the col= argument.

#| exercise: ex_hist_col
# (one command)


add col as an additional argument here and give it a color of your choice.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_hist_col
#| solution: true
hist(ToothGrowth$len, col = "steelblue")




#| exercise: ex_hist_col
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
line <- paste(.user_code, collapse = "\n")
if (!inherits(.result, "histogram")) fail("Use hist(...) on ToothGrowth$len.")

expr <- try(parse(text = line)[[1]], silent = TRUE)
if (inherits(expr, "try-error")) fail("Your code must be a single valid R expression.")
if (!(is.call(expr) && identical(as.character(expr[[1]]), "hist"))) fail("Call hist(...).")

args <- as.list(expr)[-1]
arg1_txt <- paste0(deparse(args[[1]]), collapse = "")
if (arg1_txt != "ToothGrowth$len") fail("Use ToothGrowth$len as the data argument.")

has_col <- any(names(args) == "col")
if (!has_col) fail("Add a color with col = ...")

pass("OK")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q8 — Load a CSV as a DataFrame
text:
Q8 — Load a CSV as a DataFrame
Load iris.csv into a dataframe named df.



Photo by Andrew Small on Unsplash



# Q9 SETUP — create iris.csv inside the webR filesystem
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_csv_load
write.csv(iris, "iris.csv", row.names = FALSE)



## Q9 — Load a CSV as a data frame
# Read "iris.csv" into a data frame named df
#| exercise: ex_csv_load
#| exercise.lines: 3
#| echo: false
df <- 


Use read.csv here.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_csv_load
#| solution: true
#| files: ["data/iris.csv"]   # solution runs in its own sandbox — mount again
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")




#| exercise: ex_csv_load
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  if (!exists("df", envir = .envir_result)) fail("Create `df` with `read.csv(\"iris.csv\")`.")
  x <- get("df", envir = .envir_result)
  if (!is.data.frame(x)) fail("`df` should be a data frame.")
  need <- c("Sepal.Length","Sepal.Width","Petal.Length","Petal.Width","Species")
  if (!all(need %in% names(x))) fail("`df` doesn't look like the expected columns.")
  pass("✅ Loaded correctly!")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
text:
Q9 — Count rows in iris.csv
Count the number of rows in iris.csv and store them in the variable rows

# Q10 setup
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_csv_nrows
write.csv(iris, "iris.csv", row.names = FALSE)



#| exercise: ex_csv_nrows
#| exercise.lines: 3
#| echo: false
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")
rows <- 


Use nrow.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_csv_nrows
#| solution: true
#| files: ["data/iris.csv"]   # solution runs in its own sandbox — mount again
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")
rows <- nrow(df)




#| exercise: ex_csv_nrows
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  stopifnot(exists("rows", envir=.envir_result))
  x <- get("rows", envir=.envir_result)
  if (!is.numeric(x) || length(x)!=1) fail("`rows` must be a single number.")
  if (x==150) pass("✅ 150 rows.") else pass("ℹ️ Count recorded.")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
text:
Q10 — Show column names
Print the column names from iris.csv

#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_csv_names
write.csv(iris, "iris.csv", row.names = FALSE)



#| exercise: ex_csv_names
#| exercise.lines: 4
#| echo: false
# Read the CSV and print the column names
df   <- read.csv("iris.csv")
cols <- 



Use names.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_csv_names
#| solution: true
#| files: ["data/iris.csv"]   # solution runs in its own sandbox — mount again
df   <- read.csv("iris.csv")
cols <- names(df)
cols



#| exercise: ex_csv_names
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  if (!exists("cols", envir=.envir_result)) fail("Create `cols <- names(df)` and print it.")
  x <- get("cols", envir=.envir_result)
  if (!is.character(x)) fail("`cols` should be a character vector from `names(df)`.")
  need <- c("Sepal.Length","Sepal.Width","Petal.Length","Petal.Width","Species")
  if (!all(need %in% x)) fail("Those don’t look like the iris columns—did you read the CSV and call `names(df)`?")
  pass("✅ Columns detected: Sepal/ Petal and Species.")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q11 — Print some of the data
text:
Q11 — Print some of the data
Print the first 5 rows from iris.csv.

#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_csv_head
write.csv(iris, "iris.csv", row.names = FALSE)



#| exercise: ex_csv_head
#| exercise.lines: 4
#| echo: false
# Read the CSV and print the column names
df   <- read.csv("iris.csv")
#your code here



Use head.


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_csv_head
#| solution: true
#| files: ["data/iris.csv"]   # solution runs in its own sandbox — mount again
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")
head(df)



#| exercise: ex_csv_head
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  # If the *last* expression is head(df, 5), .result should be a 5-row data.frame
  if (!(is.data.frame(.result) && nrow(.result)==5)) {
    fail("Show the first 5 rows (e.g., `head(df, 5)`) as the **last** line.")
  }
  if (!all(c("Sepal.Length","Sepal.Width","Petal.Length","Petal.Width","Species") %in% names(.result))) {
    fail("Your preview should be from `df` read from the CSV (it must have the iris columns).")
  }
  pass("✅ Nice—now you can clearly see the columns and a few rows.")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q12 — Make a histogram from iris.csv
text:
Q12 — Make a histogram from iris.csv
Make a histogram of the column Sepal.Length from iris.csv.

#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_csv_hist
write.csv(iris, "iris.csv", row.names = FALSE)



#| exercise: ex_csv_hist
#| exercise.lines: 3
#| echo: false
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")



Use hist (see Q5).


Solution. 

#| exercise: ex_csv_hist
#| solution: true
#| files: ["data/iris.csv"]   # solution runs in its own sandbox — mount again
df <- read.csv("iris.csv")
hist(df$Sepal.Length, col = "lightgray", main = "Sepal.Length", xlab = "cm") 



#| exercise: ex_csv_hist
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this({
  if (!inherits(.result,"histogram")) fail("Use base `hist(df$Sepal.Length, ...)`.")
  if (!grepl("Sepal\\.Length", paste(.user_code, collapse="\n"))) fail("Plot `df$Sepal.Length`.")
  pass("✅ Histogram detected.")
})

title: Graded Tutorial: Introduction to R
section: Q13 — Write a function using division

title: Tutorial: Introduction to R
text:
Objective Learn how to compute summary statistics and create basic plots using dplyr and ggplot2, two core packages from the tidyverse.

Setup


Load necessary packages

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Example Data

We’ll use the built-in mtcars dataset for demonstration.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Summary Statistics

Calculate mean, median, standard deviation, and count for mpg (miles per gallon).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
You can also group by a categorical variable, e.g., number of cylinders (cyl):

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Basic Plots with ggplot2

Scatter plot of mpg vs wt (weight):

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Boxplot of mpg by number of cylinders:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Exercise (Optional)

Compute the mean horsepower (hp) for each cylinder group and create a bar plot of these means.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: An Activity in R Introducing Visual
text: This script will provide a quick demonstration of summarizing data visually.

title: An Activity in R Introducing Visual
text:
Pixar Data

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The data we will be looking at contains information about Pixar films released prior to 2021, as provided by Wikipedia. The following variables were collected for each film:

film: The name of the film.
release_date: The date the film premiered.
run_time: The length of the film in minutes.
rotten_tomatoes: Score from the review-aggregation website Rotten Tomatoes; scored out of 100.
box_office_worldwide: Box office gross amount in U.S. dollars worldwide.
budget: Movie budget in U.S. dollars.

The data can be found in the following table:

Descriptive Statistics
The following functions are useful for summarizing numerical data: mean, median, min, max, range, sd, var, fivenum
Try them here:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Basic Plots
There is a generic plot function in R which is useful for creating quick visualizations of a data set. If you pass one numeric variable into this function, a quick plot will be made by creating a point for each observation in the order they occur in the data frame (index). Passing two numeric variables into this function will create a scatterplot, with the first variable corresponding to the x-axis and the second corresponding to the y-axis.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There are some arguments we can add to the plot function to make our visualizations more informative:

main = “Title of the Graph”
xlab = “x-axis label”
ylab = “y-axis label”
xlim = c(0,100) (changing the values on the x-axis)
ylim = c(0,100) (changing the values on the y-axis)
col = “colour” (full list of colours can be found here)
pch = number (list of potential shapes for points can be found here)

We can even add text to our plots by incorporating the text function.


Barplots
To draw a basic bar graph, we can simply use the barplot function and specify which variable it is that we want to represent the height of the bars. For example, we can use the following code to quickly plot the Rotten Tomatoes score for each of the Pixar movies:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There are some arguments we can add to the barplot function to make our bar graph more informative.

horiz = T (draw the bars horizontally)
name = variable to label the bars
las = 2 (rotates the labels)
cex.names = 0.5 (change the labels font size)



Histograms
We can create histograms of the data using the hist function. For example, we can use the following code to quickly create a histogram of the Rotten Tomatoes scores for the Pixar movies:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Arguments we can add to the hist function to make our graph more informative.

breaks = number (number of bars displayed)
labels = T (add numbers to bars)



Boxplots
The boxplot function can be used to (unsurprisingly) create boxplots. Keeping with our Rotten Tomatoes examples:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Arguments we can add to the boxplot function to make our graph more informative.

horizontal = T (draw the boxplot horizontally)

title: An Activity in R on Coin Flipping
text:
Coin Flip Example
For a random variable \(X\) that follows the Bernoulli distribution with probability \(p\), each realization of the random variable has two possible outcomes (e.g., Success or Failure, 1 or 0, Heads or Tails, etc.). More specifically, the Bernoulli distribution is a discrete probability distribution such that \(P(X=1)=p\) and \(P(X=0)=1-p\).
As a simple example, we can design an experiment where we flip a coin and record the outcome using the following code:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Now let’s try flipping the coin a few times:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Let’s do this again, but this time we’ll keep track of the number of Heads and Tails that occur.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
By default, the sample function gives every outcome an equal chance of happening. If we want to make certain events more likely than others, we can do this by adding a prob argument. For example, we can make it so that our coin has a 75% chance of landing on heads for any single flip by using the following code:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Finally, let’s try flipping the coin 100 times and keep track of how the frequency of Heads progresses over the tosses.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
